_note: Canonical entity registry. Types match the controlled vocabulary in triples.json. 'parent' expresses subtopic relationships. 'definition' is a term-definition pair.

g-w-f-hegel.label: G W F Hegel
g-w-f-hegel.definition: 19th-century German philosopher, originator of dialectical thinking and the thesis-antithesis-synthesis framework.

andrej-karpathy.label: Andrej Karpathy
andrej-karpathy.definition: AI researcher and former Tesla/OpenAI engineer, known for influential public thinking on LLMs and learning.

harry-g-frankfurt.label: Harry G. Frankfurt
harry-g-frankfurt.definition: Moral philosopher, author of On Bullshit, which defines bullshit as indifference to the truth-falsity distinction.

paul-grice.label: Paul Grice
paul-grice.definition: Philosopher of language who formulated the cooperative principle and conversational maxims.

bender-and-gebru.label: Bender and Gebru
bender-and-gebru.definition: Emily Bender and Timnit Gebru, co-authors of the 2021 Stochastic Parrots paper on the risks of large language models. (Combined form retained for backward compatibility — see individual entries.)

emily-bender.label: Emily Bender
emily-bender.definition: Computational linguist at the University of Washington, co-author of the Stochastic Parrots paper.

timnit-gebru.label: Timnit Gebru
timnit-gebru.definition: AI ethics researcher, founder of DAIR Institute, co-author of the Stochastic Parrots paper.

herbert-clark.label: Herbert Clark
herbert-clark.definition: Psycholinguist at Stanford, co-author of the foundational Grounding in Communication framework (1991).

susan-brennan.label: Susan Brennan
susan-brennan.definition: Cognitive scientist, co-author with Herbert Clark of the grounding in communication framework.

lucy-suchman.label: Lucy Suchman
lucy-suchman.definition: Anthropologist and computer scientist, author of Plans and Situated Actions (1987), arguing that human action is shaped by concrete situations rather than pre-formed plans.

helena-rodemann.label: Helena Rodemann
helena-rodemann.definition: Design researcher who argues that personality in chat agents constitutes a form of emotional labor.

jakob-nielsen.label: Jakob Nielsen
jakob-nielsen.definition: Usability researcher and co-founder of Nielsen Norman Group, known for heuristic evaluation and foundational web usability research.

don-norman.label: Don Norman
don-norman.definition: Cognitive scientist and designer, co-founder of Nielsen Norman Group, author of The Design of Everyday Things.

jasper-ai.label: Jasper.ai
jasper-ai.definition: AI writing assistant tool, one of the early consumer products for AI-assisted content generation.

conversational-interface.label: Conversational interface
conversational-interface.definition: Any interface where interaction is mediated through natural language exchange: chatbots, voice assistants, LLM chat interfaces.

conversational-ai.label: Conversational AI
conversational-ai.definition: AI systems designed to conduct natural language dialogue. Encompasses all chatbots, but also voice assistants, LLM-based agents, and other dialogue systems. Chatbots are a subtype.

chatbots.label: Chatbots
chatbots.definition: Text-based conversational agents, typically with a defined persona and task scope. A specific subtype of conversational AI.

llms.label: LLMs
llms.definition: Large language models: neural networks trained on large text corpora to predict and generate language.

chatgpt.label: ChatGPT
chatgpt.definition: OpenAI's conversational AI product, an LLM-based chat interface released in late 2022.

genai.label: GenAI
genai.definition: Generative AI: AI systems capable of generating text, images, code, and other content from a prompt.

agentic-ai.label: Agentic AI
agentic-ai.definition: AI systems that can autonomously plan and execute multi-step tasks, often using tools and external services.

dialectical-thinking.label: Dialectical thinking
dialectical-thinking.definition: A method of reasoning that develops understanding by confronting a position with its opposite, and resolving the tension into a synthesis.

thesis-antithesis-synthesis.label: Thesis-antithesis-synthesis
thesis-antithesis-synthesis.definition: Hegel's three-step dialectical process: begin with an initial position (thesis), confront it with a counter-position (antithesis), arrive at a synthesis that integrates both.

situated-action.label: Situated action
situated-action.definition: Suchman's concept that human action is shaped by the concrete, unfolding situation rather than by pre-formed abstract plans.

cooperative-principle.label: Cooperative principle
cooperative-principle.definition: Grice's framework: conversational participants implicitly cooperate by following four maxims — quality (be truthful), quantity (be informative), relevance (be relevant), and manner (be clear).

grices-maxim-of-quality.label: Grice's maxim of quality
grices-maxim-of-quality.definition: The cooperative principle maxim stating that speakers should only assert what they believe to be true and have adequate evidence for.

conversational-grounding.label: Conversational grounding
conversational-grounding.definition: The process by which conversation partners establish and maintain mutual understanding, through grounding acts that confirm shared meaning.

conversation-metaphor.label: Conversation metaphor
conversation-metaphor.definition: The framing of human-machine interaction as a conversation: two parties, turn-taking, natural language exchange.

delegation-metaphor.label: Delegation metaphor
delegation-metaphor.definition: An alternative framing of human-AI interaction as delegation: the human assigns a task to an agent and receives a deliverable, without ongoing turn-taking.

note-taking-metaphor.label: Note-taking metaphor
note-taking-metaphor.definition: An alternative framing in which AI interaction is an extension of note-taking within a task domain — continuous, marginal, executable — rather than a separate conversation.

accordion-editing.label: Accordion editing
accordion-editing.definition: NN/g term for the AI writing behavior of iteratively shortening or lengthening generated text through prompts.

apple-picking.label: Apple picking
apple-picking.definition: NN/g term for the behavior of selecting pieces of previously generated content to use as context or material for a new prompt.

epistemic-bias.label: Epistemic bias
epistemic-bias.definition: A systematic tendency to evaluate information in ways that preserve existing beliefs rather than revise them in light of evidence.

anthropomorphism.label: Anthropomorphism
anthropomorphism.definition: The attribution of human characteristics, emotions, or intentions to non-human entities — a default tendency activated by conversational interfaces.

cognitive-laziness.label: Cognitive laziness
cognitive-laziness.definition: The human tendency to minimize cognitive effort in communication, defaulting to trust and assuming cooperative intent rather than verifying claims.

conviction.label: Conviction
conviction.definition: A firmly held belief that someone considers part of their identity — stronger than opinion, more conscious than a core belief.

opinion.label: Opinion
opinion.definition: A personal position that is subjective and requires no reasoning or evidence to support it.

indifference-to-truth.label: Indifference to truth
indifference-to-truth.definition: The property of producing outputs without any concern for whether they are true or false — distinct from lying, which requires knowing the truth.

ground-truth.label: Ground truth
ground-truth.definition: The verified, factual state of reality that claims can be measured against — absent in LLM outputs, which are generated without access to it.

metacognition.label: Metacognition
metacognition.definition: Thinking about one's own thinking: awareness, monitoring, and regulation of cognitive processes.

articulation-barrier.label: Articulation barrier
articulation-barrier.definition: NN/g term for the usability threshold in generative AI: to get what you want, you must first be able to express it precisely in a prompt — a skill that is not trivial.

cognitive-load.label: Cognitive load
cognitive-load.definition: The mental effort required to process information and complete a task — increases when interfaces force non-linear work into linear formats.

direct-manipulation.label: Direct manipulation
direct-manipulation.definition: Shneiderman's interaction design principle: users should be able to act directly on objects of interest rather than through indirect commands or prompts.

context-engineering.label: Context engineering
context-engineering.definition: Karpathy's term for the practice of filling an LLM's context window with precisely the right information for the next step — reframed by Maaike as context design.

context-design.label: Context design
context-design.definition: Maaike's reframing of context engineering: the deliberate, structured design of the information that shapes an LLM's behavior — an evolution of information architecture applied to AI.

context-window.label: Context window
context-window.definition: The working memory of an LLM: the total input (prompt, history, system instructions, retrieved context) available to the model at inference time.

system-prompt.label: System prompt
system-prompt.definition: The instruction set passed to an LLM before the user turn, defining its behavior, constraints, persona, and context.

semantic-information-types.label: Semantic information types
semantic-information-types.definition: Structured content building blocks with clear semantic labels (instructions, prerequisites, warnings, examples) — the design vocabulary for context engineering.

information-architecture.label: Information architecture
information-architecture.definition: The structural design of information environments: organizing, labelling, and structuring content so it is findable, usable, and meaningful.

instructional-design.label: Instructional design
instructional-design.definition: The systematic design of learning experiences and materials, using structured content types to support understanding and task completion.

information-mapping.label: Information mapping
information-mapping.definition: A structured writing methodology that organizes content into typed, labelled blocks (procedures, concepts, facts) for clarity and reuse.

topic-based-writing.label: Topic-based writing
topic-based-writing.definition: A technical writing approach that organizes content into self-contained, typed topics (task, concept, reference) that can be reused across contexts.

technical-authoring.label: Technical authoring
technical-authoring.definition: The professional practice of creating structured, accurate, user-focused documentation for technical products and systems.

rhetoric.label: Rhetoric
rhetoric.definition: Language designed to persuade through stylistic, emotional, or structural means — may resemble an argument but does not require logical validity.

argument.label: Argument
argument.definition: A logical structure consisting of premises, reasoning, and a conclusion — builds a case in one direction without inherent claim to factual truth.

bullshit.label: Bullshit
bullshit.definition: Frankfurt's term: speech or output that is produced without concern for truth or falsity. The bullshitter is not lying — they simply do not care whether what they say is true.

designing-for-doubt.label: Designing for doubt
designing-for-doubt.definition: A design approach that builds skepticism, source-tracking, and fact-checking capacity into conversational AI interfaces — countering the user's natural trust in conversational form.

conversation-design.label: Conversation design
conversation-design.definition: The discipline of designing interactions between humans and conversational interfaces — scripting, flow, persona, and error handling.

emotional-labor.label: Emotional labor
emotional-labor.definition: The management of feeling to create a publicly observable expression — applied to chat agents that are designed to perform relational and emotional work.

stochastic-parrot.label: Stochastic parrot
stochastic-parrot.definition: Bender and Gebru's term for LLMs: systems that generate plausible-sounding text by stochastically stitching together patterns, without any understanding of meaning.

llm-hallucinations.label: LLM hallucinations
llm-hallucinations.definition: Outputs by LLMs that are confidently stated but factually incorrect — a structural consequence of probabilistic word prediction without access to ground truth.

confabulation.label: Confabulation
confabulation.definition: The filling of knowledge gaps with plausible-sounding fabrications — a more precise term for LLM hallucinations, borrowed from neuropsychology where it describes unconscious gap-filling without intent to deceive.

probabilistic-word-prediction.label: Probabilistic word prediction
probabilistic-word-prediction.definition: The core mechanism of LLMs: given a context, predict the most likely next token based on patterns in training data.

missing-fundamental.label: Missing fundamental
missing-fundamental.definition: An acoustic phenomenon where the perceived pitch of a sound is absent from the actual signal — used as a metaphor for how LLMs produce outputs that sound grounded without containing ground truth.
